01_initial_request.target_name: Dual-Consensus-Based Distributed Frequency Control for Multiple Energy Storage Systems
01_initial_request.input_data: Isolated power system with N distributed battery energy storage systems (BESSs); each BESS modeled as first-order system P_Bi = K_i/(1+T_i·s)·u_i; BESS parameters (capacities, SoC ranges, time constants); power system frequency dynamics with reheat thermal generator; communication graph among BESSs; frequency deviation thresholds (±0.02Hz dead zone, ±0.2Hz limits); initial SoC values for 10 BESSs.
01_initial_request.user_intent: To coordinate multiple distributed BESSs for proportional frequency regulation while ensuring proportional use of preserved energy capacity. Existing methods either use centralized control (single-point failure risk) or decentralized droop control (no coordination, leading to uneven energy depletion). A distributed approach is needed that ensures all BESSs contribute proportionally and exhaust their reserved capacity at approximately the same time.
01_initial_request.quantifiable_goal: 1) Improve frequency response by compressing frequency deviation Δf within ±0.2Hz; 2) Ensure proportional use of preserved BESS capacity so all BESSs reach depletion simultaneously; 3) Handle heterogeneous BESS parameters (different capacities, SoC ranges); 4) Maintain frequency regulation capability for extended duration (>250s under heavy load); 5) Estimate required global parameters using only neighbor communication.

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Known / completed: BESSs can provide fast frequency regulation. Each BESS operates in three modes: discharging (Δf > Δf_min^+), idle (dead zone), charging (Δf < Δf_min^-). The controller output follows u_i = F(System and BESS parameters)·g(Δf), where g(Δf) is a piecewise linear function of frequency deviation. [Gap] Missing / unresolved: How to determine the BESS control gain K_i and design the function F(·) to ensure proportional use of preserved energy across all BESSs with different parameters, without requiring a central coordinator. [Decision] To address gain selection and proportional energy use, defined two key parameters: (1) Static parameter β_i = K_i·τ_i/2S̄_i (relating control gain to reserved capacity) and (2) Time-varying parameter τ_d_i = 1 - (SoC_i - SoC_min_i)/S̄_i (tracking energy depletion progress). Proved that proportional use requires τ_d_i/τ_d_j = (1-τ_d_i)/(1-τ_d_j) for all i,j.

tool.name: BESS parameter analysis and proportional use criterion

parameters.bess_model: P_Bi = K_i/(1 + T_i·s)·u_i, first-order with gain K_i and time constant T_i
parameters.static_parameter: β_i = K_i·τ_i/(2·S̄_i), where S̄_i is reserved capacity percentage

02_agent_trajectory.observation: The analysis reveals that to achieve proportional use: (1) The gain K_i should be tuned proportional to S̄_i/τ_i (balancing response speed with capacity); (2) The controller must include a term f(τ_d_i, τ_d_mean) to synchronize depletion progress; (3) A decoupling term g(β_i, β_mean) is needed to separate the β dynamics from τ_d dynamics. This motivates the dual-consensus approach.

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Known / completed: The proportional use criterion has been established, requiring estimation of global parameters β_mean = (1/N)Σβ_i and τ_d_mean = (1/N)Στ_d_i. [Gap] Missing / unresolved: β_mean is a static constant while τ_d_mean is time-varying, requiring different consensus algorithms. A unified controller structure is needed. [Decision] To address this, designed the centralized controller first as a reference: u_i = g(β_i, β_mean)·f(τ_d_i, τ_d_mean)·g(Δf), where g(β_i, β_mean) = β_mean/β_i (decouples β from τ_d dynamics) and f(τ_d_i, τ_d_mean) = (1-τ_d_i)/τ_d_mean (synchronizes depletion). Proved that this achieves proportional use (Theorem 1).

parameters.gain_tuning: K_i set based on S̄_i and power rate limits

02_agent_trajectory.observation: The centralized controller achieves proportional use (Theorem 1): the ratio τ_d_i/(1-τ_d_i) converges to a constant for all BESSs, meaning all τ_d_i values reach 1 (full depletion) at approximately the same time. However, this requires global knowledge of all β_i and τ_d_i values, which is impractical for large-scale distributed BESSs.

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Known / completed: The centralized controller structure has been established with proven proportional use guarantee. [Gap] Missing / unresolved: The centralized controller requires global information (β_mean and τ_d_mean) that is unavailable in distributed settings. Need distributed estimation algorithms for both the static parameter β_mean and the time-varying parameter τ_d_mean. [Decision] To estimate β_mean (static), employed Static Average Consensus (SAC): β̂̇_i = Σ_{j∈N_i}a_{ij}(β̂_j - β̂_i), β̂_i(0) = β_i. To estimate τ_d_mean (time-varying), designed Dynamic Average Consensus (DAC): τ̂_i = υ_i + (1-τ_d_i), υ̇_i = -γυ_i + Σ_{j∈N_i}a_{ij}(τ̂_j - τ̂_i). This forms the dual-consensus approach.

parameters.sac_algorithm: β̂̇_i = Σ_{j∈N_i}a_{ij}(β̂_j - β̂_i), β̂_i(0) = β_i → converges to β_mean
parameters.convergence_guarantee: SAC: exponential convergence (Lemma 1); DAC: exponential convergence to bounded set (Lemma 2)

02_agent_trajectory.observation: The dual-consensus approach provides: (1) SAC for β_mean converges exponentially (Lemma 1); (2) DAC for τ_d_mean converges exponentially to a bounded error set that can be made arbitrarily small by increasing the coupling gains (Lemma 2); (3) The distributed controller u_i = g(β_i, β̂_i)·f(τ_d_i, τ̂_i)·g(Δf) achieves the same proportional use as the centralized controller (Theorem 2).

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Known / completed: The dual-consensus distributed controller has been designed with theoretical guarantees. [Gap] Missing / unresolved: The proposed approach needs to be validated through case studies under realistic power system conditions with multiple BESSs. [Decision] To validate the approach, conducted two case studies with 10 distributed BESSs in an isolated power system: Case 1 with sufficient generation capability and Case 2 with insufficient generation (requiring extended BESS support). Compared with methods without distributed control and with recent alternatives.

parameters.test_system: Isolated power system with reheat thermal generator + 10 distributed BESSs
parameters.case1: Sufficient generation: 0.02pu load removed, then 0.03pu added
parameters.case2: Insufficient generation: 0.02pu load with 0.01pu generation gap, generation rate constraint 3%/min

02_agent_trajectory.observation: Case 1 results: All τ_d_i values remain nearly identical (perfect proportional use); frequency deviation compressed within ±0.2Hz; SAC and DAC estimates converge rapidly. Case 2 results (more challenging): τ_d_i values converge with proposed approach but diverge without it; frequency maintained within -0.2Hz for over 250s (vs. ~170s without approach); proportional BESS output maintained throughout. The approach significantly extends the duration of effective frequency regulation.

02_agent_trajectory.thought: [Background] Known / completed: Both case studies demonstrate the effectiveness of the dual-consensus approach. [Gap] Missing / unresolved: Need to analyze the sensitivity and robustness of the approach, particularly regarding communication topology, parameter uncertainties, and the interaction between SAC/DAC convergence rates and BESS dynamics. [Decision] To complete the analysis, evaluated the effects of SAC/DAC convergence speed on control performance. Since both β̂_i and τ̂_i converge much faster than τ_d_i dynamics, the distributed controller approximates the centralized one well. Also analyzed the scalability to larger BESS networks and identified the communication overhead.

parameters.convergence_rate_analysis: SAC/DAC convergence rates >> τ_d_i dynamics → good approximation
parameters.scalability: Communication overhead depends on graph connectivity, not network size
parameters.robustness: Proportional use maintained despite parameter uncertainties in BESS models

02_agent_trajectory.observation: The dual-consensus approach is robust and scalable: 1) The SAC/DAC convergence rates can be tuned independently of BESS dynamics; 2) Only neighbor-to-neighbor communication is needed, making it suitable for large-scale deployments; 3) The approach naturally handles heterogeneous BESS parameters; 4) Future work includes investigating non-ideal communication effects and integrating energy arbitrage into the distributed control framework.

03_success_verification.validation_technique: Theoretical analysis with exponential convergence proofs for SAC (Lemma 1) and DAC (Lemma 2), and proportional use guarantee for the distributed controller (Theorem 2), validated through two comprehensive case studies with 10 BESSs in an isolated power system.

Frequency Deviation Compression.interpretation: Frequency deviation is maintained within the ±0.2Hz normal operating threshold, compared to -0.25Hz without BESS coordination.

Proportional Use Achievement.interpretation: All BESS τ_d_i values remain nearly identical, ensuring proportional use of preserved capacity and simultaneous depletion.

Frequency Regulation Duration (Case 2).interpretation: Under insufficient generation conditions, the proposed approach maintains frequency within limits for over 250s, vs. ~170s without coordination.

SAC Convergence.interpretation: Static average consensus for β_mean estimation converges exponentially fast to the true average value.

DAC Tracking Error.interpretation: Dynamic average consensus for τ_d_mean estimation converges to a bounded error set that can be made arbitrarily small by increasing coupling gains.

03_success_verification.final_verdict: The dual-consensus-based distributed control approach successfully coordinates multiple BESSs for proportional frequency regulation. By separately estimating static (β_mean via SAC) and dynamic (τ_d_mean via DAC) parameters, the approach ensures all BESSs proportionally use their preserved capacity, maximizing the total available capacity for frequency regulation. Case studies demonstrate significant improvements in both frequency response quality and regulation duration compared to uncoordinated approaches.
